I think that GDBus automatically shares the default connection between all Bus objects - as described in [g_bus_get_sync](https://developer.gnome.org/gio/stable/GDBusConnection.html#g-bus-get-sync)

I guess you can create another true connection with [g_dbus_connection_new_for_address_sync](https://developer.gnome.org/gio/stable/GDBusConnection.html#g-dbus-connection-new-for-address-sync) ([Gio.DBusConnection.new_for_address_sync](https://people.gnome.org/~gcampagna/docs/Gio-2.0/Gio.DBusConnection.new_for_address_sync.html)) - and then give it to the Bus object:

```python
from gi.repository import Gio

bus = SessionBus()
bus.con = Gio.DBusConnection.new_for_address_sync(address, None, None, None, None)
```

Still, to solve it cleanly I'll need to add an official support for connecting to a given address.

I like the idea of a declarative API (and discussing the API from the start!). From what I understand a declarative API means the user writes code describing *what* they want, rather than *how* to do it (imperative). Thus I think it would work out best if we start with a clear path between basic declarations and what they mean. Are there any common practices out there?

Building on your `BasePlot` snippet, maybe it makes sense to use `key_name` to define groupings and separate them from the corresponding visual component (color, linewidth, etc.). Then the user could say "I want my node groups to be different colors and my edge groups to be different linewidths" like so:

```python
b = BasePlot(G, node_grouping=('key_name', 'color'), edge_grouping=('key_name', 'linewidth'))
```

We could use matplotlib's [`cycler`](http://matplotlib.org/cycler/) API under the hood.

I also agree this could get messy too. We'd need a way to determine if the values under the keyed attribute are compatible with the visual element being styled (e.g. a continuous-valued attribute is compatible with a sequential colormap but not a linestyle).

Hi! The documentation definitely needs improvement. :)

The idea of having both is flexibility when using the CLI for controlling ZAP. `active-scan` only runs an active scan against a URL that is already in ZAP's site tree, it does nothing else, and so is intended for use after other commands. Whereas `quick-scan` is intended to be a way to run quick scans with most options contained within it (including being able to start and shutdown ZAP before and after), so you can do everything in one go. Without any other options passed to the command, `quick-scan` would both open the URL to make sure it's in the site tree, run an active scan, and will always output any found alerts of the given level. So, as an example, this quick-scan command from the README:
```
zap-cli quick-scan --scanners xss,sqli --spider --recursive --exclude "some_regex_pattern" http://127.0.0.1/
```

Is equivalent to running:
```
zap-cli open-url http://127.0.0.1/
zap-cli exclude "some_regex_pattern"
zap-cli spider http://127.0.0.1/
zap-cli active-scan --scanners xss,sqli --recursive http://127.0.0.1/
zap-cli alerts
```

Does that make sense? I'll add some docs about it to the README and `--help` output and CC you on the PR for feedback. :)

@cocagne,

Thanks for the comment. I'm very conservative with changes and code/systems in general, thus I share some of the concerns @oberstet raised.

Being only slightly familiar with the codebase I can confirm at least that the current tests cover the full native round-trip Python > DBus > Python, but only among the same Python versions, *not* across different ones. These are implemented in `client_tests.py` and driven by `test_client_against_native_bus.py`. Interestingly, an alternative driver for such tests, bypassing communication with a system provided DBus is implemented in `test_client_against_internal_bus.py` and is currently disabled (I tried enabling it, but found no quick solution for succesfully running this complementary set of tests).

What drove me to improve txdbus is a very particular use case of using RFCOMM bluetooth sockets on a Raspberry PI: this is done over DBus, talking to the services provided by BlueZ, and working fine for me.

Having said this, I suggest that:
* The test @oberstet identified  (`test_long`) should be reviewed before closing this -- the current implementation is currently running and passing on Python 3 which does not make much sense, given that it's doing nothing. It should be skipped instead.
* Line https://github.com/cocagne/txdbus/blob/master/txdbus/router.py#L47 really seems to contain a reference to an unknown variable `pn`... I have no idea what that is, but feel this *needs* fixing -- not related to Python 3. Maybe a new issue should be created instead.
* With regards to improving the codebase via linting and increased coverage, again, I feel should be handled progressively under independent issues / PRs.

This approach ensures each issue / PR is as focused as possible and brings in the smallest amount of changes at a time -- I guess I can all agree that is a good policy. :)

In short:
* Create a new issue skip the `test_long` in Python 3 and call this ticket complete once that lands.
* Create new issue to fix the "unknown variable" @oberstet identified in `router.py` -- unrelated to Python 3 -- @cocagne, maybe you can help out a bit here if this is still familiar to you?
* Create many issues -- maybe one per module? -- to improve linting results.
* Create one issue to prepare for a new PyPI release -- updating `setup.py` and associated metadata.
* Release, letting people out there use txdbus on Python 3. Now that there's better infrastructure in place, any regressions should be easier to identify and address. :)

When cffi is missing oscrypto falls back to ctypes. In this case that happens to fail, as the line `libcrypto.EVP_MD_CTX_new.argtypes = []` raises **AttributeError: Unable to resolve symbol**.

Seeing that you use OpenBSD I assume you're using LibreSSL, so could this be some kind of LibreSSL vs. OpenSSL thing? It seems that LibreSSL doesn't have EVP_MD_CTX_new, instead there's EVP_MD_CTX_create (see [evp.h](https://github.com/libressl-portable/openbsd/blob/master/src/lib/libcrypto/evp/evp.h#L540)). Based on the related lines in [_libcrypto_ctypes.py](https://github.com/wbond/oscrypto/blob/master/oscrypto/_openssl/_libcrypto_ctypes.py#L249-L264) oscrypto *does* support both EVP_MD_CTX_new and EVP_MD_CTX_create, but picks one based on a version check. Maybe the check is the problem?

Just an idea: Instead of an explicit version check oscrypto could try to access libcrypto.EVP_MD_CTX_new and try the *_create variant if there's an AttributeError.

Hi @Moondra ! You are almost there.

The callback should take a single argument, a "keyboard event" object. This is why the error says "3 required positional arguments": because `hook` only passed one argument (the event object), and your function expected four.

It should look more like this (untested):

```
def printer(event):
    print (event.name)
    if event.name == 'down':
        print('x')

keyboard.hook(printer)
```

But note this will print twice, once for the KEY_DOWN event and once for the KEY_UP event, so you may want to filter by `event_type` too.

Good luck!

Sure! So, for sure this is one of the most immediate friction points one would hit, especially coming from `logging`. There are two approaches I use for printing `begin` events:

1. Emit `begin` events for transactions marked as `verbose`.
2. Emit `begin` events for transactions with above a certain level (e.g., >debug in your case)

You can see the first approach in action in [Chert](https://github.com/mahmoud/chert/blob/73c02762b181213e0059ea09f842c95b90708a41/chert/log.py#L15-L34). Note the separate complete format (`fmt`) and begin format (`begin_fmt`). This gives an output like:

```
b+0.277090 --------------    "render site beginning"
F+0.277154 -    0.008ms -       "no pre_render hook defined"
b+0.278630 --------------       "render published entry content beginning"
S+1.213840 -  935.210ms -       "render published entry content succeeded - ({'verbose': True})"
b+1.214119 --------------       "render draft entry content beginning"
S+2.776296 - 1562.177ms -       "render draft entry content succeeded - ({'verbose': True})"
b+2.776552 --------------       "render special entry content beginning"

S+2.892354 - 2615.264ms -    "render site succeeded - ({'verbose': True})"
```
I'm not super happy about the `({'verbose': True})`, but it works pretty well thus far. Enough so that I've made verbose a special part of `SensibleFilter`.

I think you mix up [Update](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#update) and [Message](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#message). The former encapsulates the latter. The Bot API method `getUpdate` gives you Update objects, but telepot's `message_loop` would strip the "outer layer" and give you the Message object within.

`per_chat_id` (and most other functions in telepot) acts on Message, not Update.

Strictly speaking, an Update may encapsulate one of five types of objects. Those are reflected in their flavors:
- Message (flavor `chat`)
- Message (flavor `edited_chat`)
- [CallbackQuery](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#callbackquery) (flavor `callback_query`)
- [InlineQuery](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#inlinequery) (flavor `inline_query`)
- [ChosenInlineResult](https://core.telegram.org/bots/api#choseninlineresult) (flavor `chosen_inline_result`)

Everywhere in the documentations, I use the generic noun "message" to refer to all these types of objects. I hope the context and upper/lower-case of the first letter prevent any ambiguity.

By reading the title "per_chat_id() doesn't play well with callback queries", I think I can guess where your real problem lies. When you have a `ChatHandler` to deal with a private chat, you expect it to capture both of these:

1. a Message whose `msg['chat']['id']` is a certain number
2. a CallbackQuery whose `msg['from']['id']` is also that certain number

As of today (version 8.1), `ChatHandler` does exactly that, by default. (I am updating the docs. Please look at the source code for now.)

Almost all the time, `ChatHandler` is paired with `per_chat_id`, which causes a new `ChatHandler` to be born for every new `msg['chat']['id']` seen, matching case number (1) above. However, `per_chat_id` does not do the same for every new `msg['from']['id']` seen for CallbackQuery, creating an asymmetry with case number (2). (Please don't feel bad if you don't fully understand what I am saying. This issue requires a deeper understanding of telepot.)

It will be fixed in an upcoming version. I will also update documentations in light of all the recent changes of Bot API.

The suggested explanation doesn't make any sense for Python 3.2 users, where `unicode` does not exist and `str` is a character string type. Indeed, even in 2.x, `bytes is str`, so it's much more precise to refer to byte string objects as `bytes`.

I have no intentions of supporting people to write 2.x-only code in 2015. I also see little reason in enabling dangerous code in the first place. I can certainly imagine code for a firewall going like
```
with open('config', 'rb') as f:
    for line in f:
        ipaddress.ip_address(line.strip())
```
Such code yields different results under 2.x and 3.x with the two linked backports, without any kind of warning. Since one major usage scenario of ipaddress is security-related stuff, linking to an implementation with a security hole waiting to happen does not seem to be a good idea.

It was a design choice. A badly formatted token is something that can be checked before sending so I chose to raise an exception if any of the tokens are invalid. It's similar to validating that a `list` contains all strings and raising an exception vs. silently removing the non-strings from the list.

The client-user can choose to remove the invalid tokens if that is the behavior they want by filtering invalid tokens using that method before sending.

I think the behavior needs to stay as it is.  As you pointed out, it would be consistent with other database libraries.  It would also be consistent with early copies of [PEP 343](https://docs.python.org/2.5/whatsnew/pep-343.html).  Note the example does exactly what everyone is doing.

I want to reiterate that I feel they are completely unnecessary.  I use pyodbc in multiple 24x7 servers in the financial industry and have never used a context manager with it, or with anything else like files.  I gave examples of how you could write code without a context manager which is much clearer.  (And that is what I meant about people getting confused - not the action of the context manager itself, but the fact that they are completely unnecessary in C Python today.)

For example, this code from your post would never be written that way:

```python
with file('/tmp/myfile', 'w') as f:
    f.write('hello\n')
```

You would just do this:

```python
open('/tmp/myfile', 'w').write('hello\n')
```

You'll see code like this all the time.  (I know your example was shortened and there are usually more lines written, but stick with me.)  Objects in Python are deleted automatically and the all "do the right thing" when they are deleted.  Files close.  Sockets close.  Database connections rollback and close.

In a longer function with more lines, it would look like this:

```python
def f():
    fd = open('/tmp/myfile', 'w')
    fd.write('1\n')
    fd.write('2\n')
```

The file *will* be closed as soon as the function is exited, either normally at the bottom or if an exception occurs.

The only time it could be useful is in a loop where you catch exceptions.  In that case you can eliminate a `finally` clause:

```python
def f():
    while x > y:
        try:
            data = wait_for_data()
            with fd as open('filename', 'w'):
                fd.write(data + '\n')
       except:
           logger.error('Error occurred', exc_info=True)
```

In this case `fd` does get closed before the next iteration calls `wait_for_data`.

In my servers connections are primarily used in two patterns:

In "task loops", a connection is created and used by each task in the queue.  When an error occurs, the error is logged and the connection is closed as part of the cleanup.  A new one is allocated and the process continues.  This type of code is too complex for a `with` since there is a lot of code.  There aren't just a couple of lines to indent.

The second type is a function allocates a connection, uses it, then commits, just like the examples from my previous post.  These are very clean and easy.  Connections and cursors are closed automatically:

```python
def somefunction():
    cursor = pyodbc.connect('mydsn').cursor()
    cursor.execute("delete something")
    cursor.commit()
```

I recommend giving this a try in a couple of places and see if it works for you.  It is one of the things I greatly prefer about Python over Javascript and Java.  Both of those languages can use context managers.  (C++ and C# support object types on the stack that run destructors, so a simple "wrapper" object would be just as easy.)

In general it's a difficult thing to do in Python because rendering details like font metrics are platform specific and are not well supported by available libraries (at least at the time that bit was written).

Hi Arpit,

Indeed, that would be the way to do this. `lib.xpathhelper` might really help you here, since when `--use-xpathhelper` is used as a command-line switch, and a `YANGPathHelper` is provided, then pyangbind maintains a pseudo-XML document internally so that XPATH expressions can be handled.

This is a `lxml.etree` instance, which starts with an element called `root`. You can see it by calling `yang_path_helper_instance.tostring(pretty_print=True)`. Essentially, it looks something like:

```xml
<root>
  <test obj_ptr="ee615914-d975-11e5-910f-acbc32aad1a5">
    <number-range obj_ptr="ee62ffdc-d975-11e5-93ac-acbc32aad1a5"/>
    <ll obj_ptr="ee630c63-d975-11e5-a043-acbc32aad1a5"/>
    <number obj_ptr="ee631378-d975-11e5-a29e-acbc32aad1a5"/>
  </test>
  <load-one obj_ptr="ee631a38-d975-11e5-a78c-acbc32aad1a5">
    <item obj_ptr="ee631f80-d975-11e5-8a62-acbc32aad1a5"/>
  </load-one>
  <load-two obj_ptr="ee6329d9-d975-11e5-b5a4-acbc32aad1a5">
    <item obj_ptr="ee6332c2-d975-11e5-bceb-acbc32aad1a5"/>
  </load-two>
</root>
```

The `obj_ptr` attribute is a key that is used in a dictionary named `_library` of the same class. This provides a pointer to the pyangbind class that represents that element. To write a serialiser, one really just needs to take elements that are leaves, and translate those across to their actual value, I think.

Here's a 5-min PoC:

```python
from pyangbind.lib.xpathhelper import YANGPathHelper
from tmp import test
import copy
from lxml import etree

p = YANGPathHelper()
t = test(path_helper=p)

print p.tostring(pretty_print=True)

new_tree = copy.deepcopy(p._root)

for elem in new_tree.iter("*"):
    if "obj_ptr" in elem.attrib:
        if p._library[elem.attrib["obj_ptr"]]._is_leaf:
            elem.text = unicode(p._library[elem.attrib["obj_ptr"]])
        else:
            pass
        del elem.attrib["obj_ptr"]

print etree.tostring(new_tree, pretty_print=True)                                                  
```

This outputs:

```xml
<root>
  <test>
    <number-range>0</number-range>
    <ll/>
    <number>0</number>
  </test>
  <load-one>
    <item>0</item>
  </load-one>
  <load-two>
    <item>0</item>
  </load-two>
</root>
```

There are a few things that would need to be fixed -- for example, this doesn't handle leaf-lists correctly (see the `ll` element) and one would want to get rid of the `root` element. Also, it needs some functions that are just on the `ietf-json` branch right now to get the namespaces correct.

I'd be happy to assist/review a pull-req for this functionality if you have interest in working on it.

Cheers,

Nope, it's one thread per client, held until the response is complete.
Response data is buffered in-memory as fast as it can be created, and sent
to the client as fast as it can be transmitted.

This is necessary, as it's important that the same thread is used to send
the data to the same client. Consider thread local data, for example. This
means that each iterable would have to be pinned to a specific worker
thread, rather than being given to the next available worker in a pool.

Now, imagine if you've got ten iterables, all pinned to a single worker
thread, and taking it in turns to have their next() method called. Now
imagine of the the next() methods takes five minutes to run. You've just
blocked nine other responses!

Hence, the worker thread gets it's work done as fast as possible for a
single iterable, then moves onto a new request. This is identical to how
nginx acts as a reverse proxy to a gunicorn worker. Aiohttp is effectively
acting as a reverse proxy to a wsgi app.

Wsgi is not designed to be used with streaming responses. Use aiohttp for
that. The whole point of aiohttp-wsgi is that it allows sync frameworks
(Django, flask), to be used alongside async functionality (websockets,
streaming media). It's not a magic bullet for making a sync protocol like
wsgi scale to hundreds of clients.

Regarding your question of adding this operation to the library, my approach in designing the API was to make a general-purpose data structure. I realized that there were hundreds of useful methods I could add that would help in certain special situations. However, most of them would be one-liners like the above, and most would not be very adaptable. In addition, this would clutter the documentation with many confusingly similar methods, which is already a problem, as you noticed.

Rather than implement them all, I have only been including methods that could be used as a basis to make writing these one-liners easy.

That said, I do make exceptions for methods that require access to the internals of the tree, or are sufficiently complicated to write and are general enough in purpose that it would help lots of different programs.

I'd love to hear about further improvements you'd like me to implement! It makes me really happy that my library is useful, and I'd love to make it even more helpful! Whenever I get a message like this from people like you, it really makes my day!

Hi arol, I'd suggest you take a look at [django-rest-framework-social-oauth2](https://github.com/PhilipGarnero/django-rest-framework-social-oauth2/) which integrates this library together with [django-oauth-toolkit](https://django-oauth-toolkit.readthedocs.org/) into DRF.

In SPA you probably want to have your own authentication provider built in your backend which is able to generate tokens for your users (based on either good-old username/password credentials or) **as an exchange for Linkedin token**. Your frontend (JS) shall then be responsible for obtaining an access_token from Linkedin first, and then subsequently using the `convert_token` method to retrieve your App's token from your backend. Since your frontend will use the new token for all subsequent requests against your backend API, you'll also avoid hitting Linkedin API with every request.

Well it's just that if you deserialize an object (rather than initialize an object and then update it explicitly from some data) you're open to somebody giving you a shady pickled file and when you restore it, you don't necessarily know for sure that it's not going to contain a malicious object. Ideally, the serialize function will rip open the TDigest details, and save them in plaintext instead of requiring the deserialization to literally depickle into the various objects stored within the pickled file.

I don't think I explained that very well, but essentially it would be best to model the contents of a TDigest file and store that, not store the TDigest object itself. When you restore the TDigest details, you would first initialize a TDigest object and give a deserialize function that data.

When I look at a TDigest file on first glance I'm not even clear on how it's modelling the data, but there should be a centroid and count stored somehow in some lists right? Theoretically we can take all those centroid numbers and output them as JSON.

Some less relevant information, but useful maybe for understanding:
3d texture support does not exist for WebGL (version 1), therefore the image cube (with intensities) is tiled on a 2d png image https://github.com/maartenbreddels/ipyvolume/blob/master/ipyvolume/serialize.py#L54
In the alpha channel we put the intensity (scaled between 0 and 255), and the rgb colors contain the gradient (useful for lighting effect). This may be subject to change.
When the data arrives at the browser, [this code](https://github.com/maartenbreddels/ipyvolume/blob/595caaf5be93e9b3d21e35a2e925c1f37b1fe179/js/src/figure.js#L1201) sets up the shader uniforms.

More relevant:
The shaders used for [this material](https://github.com/maartenbreddels/ipyvolume/blob/595caaf5be93e9b3d21e35a2e925c1f37b1fe179/js/src/figure.js#L416) do most of the work, actually almost all of the magic is in the [fragment shader](https://github.com/maartenbreddels/ipyvolume/blob/595caaf5be93e9b3d21e35a2e925c1f37b1fe179/js/glsl/volr-fragment.glsl)
The function `sample_as_3d_texture` implements the 3d texturemapping in software, and line 122-125 is the actually computation of the colors. As you can see, the code is not super neat, it contains lots of commented out lines used for debugging, feel free to clean up some of that as well, or leave it as is.

I suggest you first try implementing something like LMIP by changing those mentioned lines, just hardcoding some values, feel free to make a PR (if you have questions) so I can see the code. 
If there are use defined values that need to be controlled, take a look how `ambient_coefficient` is handled in widgets.py, figure.js and the shader, that should give you some ideas.
I think you can support different rendering methods in the same shaders by using the preprocessor macro's, like  in the shader: https://github.com/maartenbreddels/ipyvolume/blob/595caaf5be93e9b3d21e35a2e925c1f37b1fe179/js/glsl/mesh-vertex.glsl
and by making multiple materials, like [i do here](https://github.com/maartenbreddels/ipyvolume/blob/595caaf5be93e9b3d21e35a2e925c1f37b1fe179/js/src/mesh.js#L60).

Hope this is useful, feel free to ask questions!

This can be used to scroll touch screens fairly easily. For example Android has bottom bar with right pointing triangle, circle and square. One could use any of those as an origo, then go 20 pixels up and drag 500 pixels up from there. This allows implementing scroll keywords that do not care at all what the application under test looks like, all we have to rely on is that there is the bottom bar with those three fixtures.

Or if you have a drawing app, you could use this to draw really simple pictures. Start from corner of the canvas, then move [+10px, +10px], start drawing to [+50px, +50px]. This enough is to test whether the drawing canvas registers touch correctly.

In general this is useful for creating keywords that only care about the surroundings of the thing we are testing. External application borders, canvas borders, etc.

>>> import pyVmomi

<class 'pyVmomi.VmomiSupport.vim.fault.InvalidLogin'>

What I typically do it something like:
```
try:
   WaitForTask(vm.PowerOff())
except Exception as e:
   print ("Hit %s" % e.msg)
```
That would give you message of the fault.
Sometime you'll get more details in: e.faultMessage
And some faults give more details than other, so you can look at https://code.vmware.com/apis/196/vsphere, and for the above case: [InvalidPowerState](https://code.vmware.com/apis/196/vsphere#/doc/vim.fault.InvalidPowerState.html) for more details (i.e. existingState and requestedState properties should be there as well).

And you could handle individual faults easier with try/except:
```
from pyVmomi import vim, vmodl
...

try:
   ...
except vim.fault.InvalidPowerState:
   ...
except vmodl.MethodFault: # the base of the faults
   ...
except Exception: # something unrelated to the API call
   ...
```

[Pipfile](https://github.com/pypa/pipfile), the implementation backing the parsing, can help with this:

```
import pipfile
pf = pipfile.load('LOCATION_OF_PIPFILE')
print(pf.data['default'])
```

But I wouldn’t recommend this, or depending on Pipenv in setup.py. Importing `pipenv` (or `pipfile`) means the user needs to actually install that before trying installing your package, and tools like Pipenv trying to peek into it without installing (`setup.py egg_info`) won’t work. The setup.py should only depend on Setuptools.

A middle ground solution would be to write a tool similar to [bumpversion](https://github.com/peritus/bumpversion) that automatically syncs a text file based on Pipfile. Distribute this file with your package, and read it in setup.py. Then use CI or a commit hook to make sure the files are always in sync.

Hi @davehunt thanks for the report.  This issue is related to dependency resolution across python versions when pipenv is installed using one version of python but your virtualenv is created using another.  Usually this works just fine, but occasionally when pipenv is installed with, say, python3, the resolver can have trouble resolving python2 dependencies when they are specified in `extras_require`. The [setuptools packaging documentation](https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#declaring-platform-specific-dependencies) recommends using this syntax instead:

```python
 install_requires=[
     'enum34;python_version<"3.4"',
]
```

I would recommend submitting a PR to pytest to fix this in order for the pip-tools resolver to pick it up, but the rest of the resolver issues are being tracked over in #857 so I'm going to go ahead and close this out for now. The difference is due to the different functions used in the cython code to
figure out the overlap, whether a centre of a pixel is within the aperture.
I think this can be traced back to the float precision in the corresponding
c code, and not sure it's worth tempering with. It's happening in only a
small fraction of the corner cases when the aperture goes through the
centre. The maximum difference I could make was 4 pixels, (which seem to be
significant for small apertures, but bigger differences can be made just by
positioning the aperture at a slightly different centre coordinate). Using
the method ``center`` is an approximation to begin with, and needless to
say the result won't change with the dtype of the data, etc.

I suggest that we regard this as an artefact of the float comparison, and
I'll fix the tests to use a different radius that works.

but this did not make it into the 3.0.x branch


what i'm looking for is a way to tell if a DateTime object has real timezone information (that was given intentionally)
or if the current timezone is just a "presumed" one.


* adding timezone string to constructor

```python
>>> aware = DateTime('2016/06/15 07:00:00 Europe/Vienna')
```

* convert from a timezone aware python datetime object


can i  use `timezoneNaive` for testing if the DateTime object has explicit timezone information?

the docstring suggests that it is used when converting between python datetime and zope DateTime objects.
and there might be cases where this value is None

```
print unaware.timezoneNaive.__doc__
The python datetime module introduces the idea of distinguishing
        between timezone aware and timezone naive datetime values. For lossless
        conversion to and from datetime.datetime record if we record this
        information using True / False. DateTime makes no distinction, when we
        don't have any information we return None here.
```

but for me it looks like it is something i can rely on.

it works for our unaware and aware objects

```python
>>> unaware.timezoneNaive()
True
>>> aware.timezoneNaive()
False
```

and for those converted from python datetime

```python
>>> dt_aware = pytz.timezone('Europe/Vienna').localize(datetime(2016,6,15,7,0))
>>> dt_naive = datetime(2016,6,15,7,0)
>>> DateTime(dt_naive).timezoneNaive()
True
>>> DateTime(dt_aware).timezoneNaive()
False
```

### alternatives

i can't use timezone since it always returns a GMT offset.

```python
>>> unaware.timezone()
'GMT+0'
>>> unaware2.timezone()
'GMT+2'
```

or should i check for offset information in the ISO represenatation.
it seems this can also be used to tell timezone aware and naive DateTimes apart:

```python
>>> aware.ISO()
'2016-06-15T07:00:00+02:00'
>>> unaware.ISO()
'2016-06-15T07:00:00'
>>> unaware2.ISO()
'2016-06-15T07:00:00'
```

Unfortunately I'm not familiar with connexion enough to create a proper automated test (required for a PR as far as I'm concerned) but I was able to verify a patch to connexion against a simple test case and it works. This is the patch:

```diff
diff --git a/connexion/decorators/parameter.py b/connexion/decorators/parameter.py
index 54734d8..906163b 100644
--- a/connexion/decorators/parameter.py
+++ b/connexion/decorators/parameter.py
@@ -142,6 +142,12 @@ def parameter_to_arg(parameters, consumes, function, pythonic_params=False):
             logger.debug("Body parameter '%s' in function arguments", body_name)
             kwargs[body_name] = request_body
 
+        for key, value in path_params.items():
+            if key not in kwargs:
+                kwargs[key] = value
+
         # Add query parameters
         query_arguments = copy.deepcopy(default_query_params)
         query_arguments.update({sanitize_param(k): v for k, v in request.query.items()})
```

The issue is that keyword arguments to views are silently discarded if they don't correspond to named URL placeholders which interferes with Flask-Injector because Flask-Injector uses exactly that mechanism to provide dependencies.

I hope someone can use the patch above and create a proper connexion change (if it's determined to be desired).

The App Engine Java client and Objectify do not currently support the Cloud Datastore API.

After some more pondering, I believe that the real problem is in the google/api_client gem. It uses "regular" Base64.decode64() function, while the Transaction ID is encoded with url-safe version, which uses different character set and does not add padding. So, the decoded tx value is already broken and useless. Anyway, suggested patch provides a workaround for the time being.

I am back on this patch after taking time to finish the Android library stuff. I agree that 
it looks like if we ban characters we will need to ban two. One strategy for that would
be to banning them in Address but overriding the ban in SyntheticAddress.

But that does mean we are enforcing an arbitrary ban on spec names. The suggested chars 
above include using "." and "#" but we have targets within the pants codebase that violate
those rules (e.g. antlr-3.4). I don't really like the idea of banning arbitrary chars although 
perhaps that will end up to be the best solution.

What do you think about the original idea of memoized fingerprint of the target specs? 
[Here is an implementation of that idea](https://github.com/mateor/pants/commit/f9b715b044a71ff2b919ffed9123bbbcc18e68d6), 
which prepends 12 digit sha to the spec, so the likelihood of collisions is basically nil. 
A very real cost of this strategy will be my time when running around and fixing all of the 
tests that hardcode an assumption of the current mapping. 

If the general preference is to just ban the chars then I will go ahead and implement that
instead.

The biggest problem at the moment is to create a 'Api DomainName' resource for Api Gateway through CFN. The problem is that you'd need to create it as a custom resource, but even that is not possible at the moment if you'd like to stay within Node.js 4.3 runtime, because it hasn't yet been updated to a newer version that supports the new API calls. You may be able to do it with Python though.

`AWS::ApiGateway::BasePathMapping` is created easily, but it has to have an explicit `DependsOn` for the 'Api DomainName' resource created above.

Another problem is that `AWS::Serverless::Api` does not 'export' its implicitly created `AWS::ApiGateway::Stage` resource, so you'd either have to use `AWS::ApiGateway::RestApi` directly (together with explicit `AWS::ApiGateway::Stage` and `AWS::ApiGateway::Deployment`), or rely on the undocumented `Stage` naming scheme and add `DependsOn` to `AWS::ApiGateway::BasePathMapping` for that, as described in #32.

All of the other resources are easily created through CFN, namely `AWS::CertificateManager::Certificate` and `AWS::Route53::RecordSet`.

So everything will soon be able to be done entirely through CFN, we mostly just need to wait for the Lambda runtime to be updated to the latest version.

No you're right, we were supposed to be able to version pre-releases straight from tags.

Of course, this time, we tripped over the good old `git describe` issue :tada: :smile:

Dev settings and dev dependencies have to be separated from what we distribute. On one hand, that's why we have `requirements_dev.txt`, but additionally we don't let them overflow into the main application (as with the CLI usage instructions in this PR).

The KA Lite codebase is heavily polluted with development tools, even a bundled version of Django ended up being hacked (in order to introduce a developer utility!). Lesson learned :)

I recommend that you create your own `dev.sh` script with your own favorite settings. If you want `django_extensions`, you can add it to `INSTALLED_APPS` in a local `settings.py` file.

I would add: It's nice to share tips about cool tools, but it's also sort of metawork and adding complexity to our codebase for the sake of developer setups which may be highly customized and attract a lot of discussion.

Using a basic lowest common denominator (the Django defaults) will help us to easily setup our own favorite environments.

If you have problems integrating developer utilities, then that's an issue that should be solved of course :)

Would setting `OVERLAP_COMPOUND` bit on composite glyph flag make any difference on Apple platform?

The test `width < 0` assumes integer values, but we now use floating points by default for all numeric values in interpolated UFO instances.
Since fontmake 1.4 we no longer set roundGeometry=True by default (unless --round-instances option is passed on the command line) when interpolating instances with MutatorMath (rounding actually occurs inside fontMath).
This is the commit that changed the default: https://github.com/googlei18n/fontmake/commit/d8baa40db65d599fc9eb9285e3b86353bd68f8b9

For example, in the "Noto Sans Display Medium Italic" instance, the uniFEFF glyph gets a float value for the advance width which is _almost_ zero: -8.526512829121202e-14

Instead of checking if width < 0, we should round it to integer first and then check. We round it anyway a few lines below that check because hmtx only accept integer advance widths.
When a flipped component is decomposed, the paths are revered to avoid rendering problems like you see above.

Here is the (hopefully) [correct version](https://github.com/cuthbertLab/music21/files/943835/sample_piece_redacted_v2.txt) of the MusicXML file; also see corrected screenshot below. If you run the sample Python code on this new file it turns out that `music21` still reports the offset of the last note in the bottom part as 21.0 - even though the durations of the full-measure rests have now been altered. As far as I can see this is because it ignores the actual durations of both full-measure rests and simply treats them as "breve" rests (which is what it says in the XML file), assuming that a breve rest has a duration of two whole notes (8.0 = 2 x 4.0).

> yeah but we should not make user calculate the X value by himself and by the time he calculated the X value the available resources in the cluster might change.

That is true, but it's also a rare case that only occurs when all users are sharing all of the resources of a cluster. In a multi-tenant Deis environment (which is something I've briefly mentioned in https://github.com/deis/deis/issues/2007#issuecomment-57721203), each user (or a group of users) would be given a slice of the cluster's resources which they would have access to, so the resource pool wouldn't necessarily change all that much. I still think that having a strict *and* a lazy way to scale would still be the best way to solve this.

> We should either give him an option to schedule to X or <= X

Yes, I think we're both agreeing on the same thing here. We can have both worlds with `deis scale web=20` being a **hard** scale (today's behaviour, which is what most people would expect anyways) and `deis scale web=~20` being a **soft** scale. Does that sound good to you, or is there something else missing?

It looks like TestRPCProvider implements camelCase and EthereumTesterProvider implements snake_case. My changes fix the bug when using EthereumTesterProvider, however the tests in Travis use TestRPCProvider and so they fail. 
### Proof of Concept
```py
import web3
from web3 import Web3, HTTPProvider, TestRPCProvider
from web3.providers.eth_tester import EthereumTesterProvider
from eth_tester import EthereumTester
from solc import compile_source
import sys, traceback
# Solidity source code
contract_source_code = '''
pragma solidity ^0.4.18;

contract C {
    event Test(uint indexed x);
    function test()  {
        Test(2);
    }
}
'''

compiled_sol = compile_source(contract_source_code) # Compiled source code
contract_interface = compiled_sol['<stdin>:C']
print (contract_interface['abi'])

# web3.py instance
print("======================= ETH-TESTER =======================")
eth_tester = EthereumTester()
web3 = Web3(EthereumTesterProvider(eth_tester))
accounts = eth_tester.get_accounts()
contract = web3.eth.contract(abi=contract_interface['abi'], bytecode=contract_interface['bin'])
tx_hash = contract.deploy(transaction={'from': accounts[0], 'gas': 410000})
tx_receipt = web3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
contract_address = tx_receipt['contractAddress']
contract_instance = web3.eth.contract(address=contract_address, abi=contract_interface['abi'])
transfer_filter = contract_instance.eventFilter('Test')
print (transfer_filter.get_all_entries())
tx_hash = contract_instance.transact({'from': accounts[0]}).test();
receipt = web3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
print ('Tx receipt',receipt)
try :
    print (transfer_filter.get_new_entries())
except:
    print("Failed to get events")
    print ('-'*60)
    traceback.print_exc(file=sys.stdout)
    print ('-'*60)

print("======================= END =======================\n\n")


print("======================= ETH-TESTRPC =======================")
web3 = Web3(TestRPCProvider())
accounts = web3.eth.accounts
contract = web3.eth.contract(abi=contract_interface['abi'], bytecode=contract_interface['bin'])
tx_hash = contract.deploy(transaction={'from': accounts[0], 'gas': 410000})
tx_receipt = web3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
contract_address = tx_receipt['contractAddress']
contract_instance = web3.eth.contract(address=contract_address, abi=contract_interface['abi'])
transfer_filter = contract_instance.eventFilter('Test')
print (transfer_filter.get_all_entries())
tx_hash = contract_instance.transact({'from': accounts[0]}).test();
receipt = web3.eth.getTransactionReceipt(tx_hash)
print ('Tx receipt',receipt)
try:
    print (transfer_filter.get_new_entries())
except:
    print("Failed to get events")
    print ('-'*60)
    traceback.print_exc(file=sys.stdout)
    print ('-'*60)
print("======================= END ======================")
```

The messaging is difficult, IMHO `conda` cannot forcibly prevent mixing things because some other channels do work properly with either `defaults` or `conda-forge` (as that is their - possibly unstated - intention). It's also not really possible to determine BC from inspection (it is to a limited extent, syntactically if you will, but not semantically. We don't have the tooling in place for syntactic BC determination yet). For sure, `conda create -n cf_y_env -c conda-forge Y` is much better advice than naive mixing software from each in the same env at present.

However `defaults` and `conda-forge` have very similar goals and are moving closer to each other. Some of us are very interested in figuring out how to 'fix' binary compatibility. I do not think the proprietary vs open distinction precludes making things *largely* work just fine. It is possible to define ABIs and write tooling to make it easier to stick to them and/or implement fully dynamic loading. It does need a deliberate effort though.

`conda-build` now guesses the patch level (not using the 'try-running-patch-with-21-different-levels' method in this PR though!).

I implemented this because level 1 is the standard level used nearly everywhere (and the output of git format-patch), yet conda historically only used level 0. Editing patches should never happen due to providence / credit reasons. 

I would prefer to always patch at level 1 instead of guessing because there is potential for ambiguity otherwise. In the future I would like to add a warning when the level is not 1 and eventually turn this into a fatal error and at that time remove this ugly brittle guessing code.

I was hoping to hear back from kalefranz before I spent any more time on it, to get a feel for whether they'd accept it if it passed all the tests and stuff, or if they'd prefer a different approach. If you want to dig into the CI stuff that might be mildly helpful, but I don't know if this proposal is a dead end or not.

There was a current solution to this added in #236 which still works. I was not quite happy with the solution and it is **not** documented in the Public API. I probably will add a better solution and document it while keeping the old solution working undocumented for the benefit of those who already use it.

Basically the current solution gives the user access to the path of the simulator working directory in the [pre_config](http://vunit.github.io/python_interface.html?highlight=pre_config#pre-and-post-simulation-hooks) hook under the name `simulator_output_path`. 

With the current solution there is a potential hazard with parallel simulation since all simulator processes shares the same working directory which means if you have two IP:s with identically named .mif files with different contents there can be a race condition when two tests running in parallel could end up writing the same file with different contents. 

My goal is to clean this feature up and add it to the Public API. First I will rename the `pre_config` argument to `simulator_working_directory` to better communicate the meaning. Secondly I will make sure that each parallel simulator process gets its own unique working directory. Using unique working directories a race condition can never happen since conflicting tests either execute sequentially on the same simulator process or in parallel on different simulator processes. In the first case there is exclusivity in time and in the second case there is exclusivity in space.

I'd like to come back to this now that I have a bit more experience with cockroachdb-python and SQLAlchemy.

Firstly, what you have right now is one of two correct ways to go about it.

The cockroachdb-python package' `run_transaction` function uses a nested transaction.

The SQLAlchemy's nested transaction API has two different styles in which it can be used.

The first style is as a context manager:

```
with session.begin_nested():
    ...issues a SAVEPOINT statement...
    # execute statements in the nested transaction
    session.add(...)
    session.remove(...)
    ...__exit__() issues a commit() or rollback() for you...
```

The second style is more manual. It requires one to explicitly issue exactly one `commit()` or `rollback()` instruction for every `begin_nested()` instruction.

```
session.begin_nested() # issues a SAVEPOINT statement
try:
    # execute statements in the nested transaction
    session.add(...)
    session.remove(...)
    session.commit()
except Exception:
    session.rollback()
```

The relevant quote from the SQLAlchemy docs is:

> For each begin_nested() call, a corresponding rollback() or commit() must be issued. (But note that if the return value is used as a context manager, i.e. in a with-statement, then this rollback/commit is issued by the context manager upon exiting the context, and so should not be added explicitly.)

~ http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/session_transaction.html#using-savepoint

In my case, I would like to call `commit()` and `rollback()` explicitly. As such, I have implemented a version of `run_transaction` that does not use `begin_nested()` as a context manager, but as a simple method call. The caller is then responsible for calling `commit()` or `rollback()` in the callback that is provided.

The exact code is quite tailored to our code base but I've reproduced it below anyway as it might help further the discussion.

```
def run_transaction(callback):
    # `dbsession` is a SQLAlchemy `scoped_session` object: a thread-local
    # session registry.
    # `callback` is a callable that operates directly on the same `dbsession`
    # and therefore does not take a `conn` argument the way the original
    # `run_transaction`'s callback does.
    # The `callback` is expected to call `commit()`. If a database exception is
    # raised, `rollback()` will be called by `run_transaction`.
    return _txn_retry_loop(dbsession, callback)


class _NestedTransaction(object):

    def __init__(self, session):
        self.session = session

    def __enter__(self):
        self.session.begin_nested()
        # Sessions are lazy and don't execute the savepoint
        # statement until you ask for the connection.
        self.session.connection()
        return self

    def __exit__(self, typ, value, tb):
        # Ignore any non-SQLAlchemy exceptions such as those used to trigger redirects.
        if typ is not None and issubclass(typ, sqlalchemy.exc.SQLAlchemyError):
            self.conn.rollback()


def _txn_retry_loop(session, callback):
    # Using cockroachdb it is never legal to start or end a nested
    # transaction while `savepoint_state.cockroach_restart is `False`.
    # Doing so would cause a `RELEASE SAVEPOINT {name}` or
    # `ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT {name}` instruction
    # where `name != 'cockroach_restart'`.
    #
    # As cockroach_restart is a thread-local variable it is not enough
    # to set it globally. It must be set by every thread. As such,
    # we set it here, before it gets used by any thread.
    savepoint_state.cockroach_restart = True
    with session.begin():
        while True:
            try:
                with _NestedTransaction(session):
                    return callback()
            except sqlalchemy.exc.DatabaseError as e:
                if isinstance(e.orig, psycopg2.OperationalError):
                    if e.orig.pgcode == psycopg2.errorcodes.SERIALIZATION_FAILURE:
                        continue
                raise

```

Whoa!  That's some old stuff there.  Curator has not used those versions in a _very_ long time.  Curator is now on version 4.0.6, and has some very different configuration options than you are probably seeing.  

> Name: elasticsearch
> Version: 0.4.5

The current version of the Elasticsearch python module is 2.4.0.  If you're using a version of Curator old enough to depend on something that old, you are definitely running an outdated version of Curator.

As far as the `DistributionNotFound` error is concerned, there is a FAQ answer for that [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/4.0/entrypoint-fix.html) in the official documentation.

I haven't seen the Python 2.6 deprecation notice before today, but it makes sense that you would need to upgrade to 3.5 or 2.7 if you want to be on a supported level.  Otherwise, I will do my best to continue to support 2.6 via the RPM/DEB packages described [in the documentation](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/installation.html).

@derekbekoe we tend not to have computed defaults in the _params.py file, especially randomized ones. What we do in those cases is make the default (in the signature) `None` and then do the dynamic generation in a validator.

The best place to use `default` in the `_params.py` file is when you are reflecting on an SDK methods and you want to override that default.

> In Python, it makes sense to do c.NotebookApp.server_extensions.append('my.extension'). The JSON config files cannot do this, since they are a simple dict-dump. And further, they don't generally need to, because opening and appending to a list in a JSON file is doable, unlike in Python. A further point in favor of .py files having higher priority.

We already had a long discussion on which one between py an json should take precedence and decided on Json, as we want at some point to edit configuration only through UI. Having `.py` taking precedence would mean in many cases that if a user does: `c.NotebookApp.server_extensions = ['my.extension']` no automatic tool can ever activate an extension. So which one takes precedence is still not obvious to me, and I'm not sure a single case like this one change the balance. 

Json  could perfectly have append also, as long as we decide that an append or prepend keys become a LazyAppend/LazyPrepend.

We maybe should just warn more loudly if one file erase the settings of another, or put some time in looking at tools like [redbaron](https://github.com/psycojoker/redbaron) to modify a Python config file in obvious case when a config option is **not** dynamic.

Adding to this thread my idea why it might not be working correctly:

In Jupyter Notebook, using Tornado 5.0 and above:
```python
default_loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
id(default_loop)  # 140334933252360

new_loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
id(new_loop)  # 140334932896176

asyncio.set_event_loop(new_loop)
id(asyncio.get_event_loop())  # 140334933252360
```

Notice that the event loop has not been set up, the `default_loop` persists and remains to be the event loop of choice for the `asyncio`.

In Tornado 4.5.3, when calling the last line, the `id` matches the `new_loop`, ie:

```python

...

asyncio.set_event_loop(new_loop)
id(asyncio.get_event_loop())  # 140334932896176
```

Update: sounds like we have a plan:  When creating or transferring a subdomain record, the creator also provides a list of the registrar's domain's known-missing zone files.  The registrar only incorporates the subdomain record if the list of known-missing zone files is consistent with its view of the Atlas network.  Then, the domain can tolerate the loss of prior zone files, and should they be discovered later (and invalidate any subdomains), clients can efficiently detect them and in doing so catch the registrar in a lie.

Subdomains can be updated on any zone file, but can only be created or transferred on the domain that created them.  The only reason for this constraint is to make catching the registrar in a lie efficient, since it limits the number of zone files the client needs to query.

@rsyncnet - autossh just monitors and restarts the ssh process if needed. So sshuttle creates the autossh process which creates the ssh process and this would seem to achieve the 'desired result'. And, yes, the usage would be with '-e'. *HOWEVER*, after some testing it's apparent that this *can't work* since sshuttle sends server code for compilation after the ssh session is established. In this scenario sshuttle has no knowledge of the reconnect and it does not resend the server code. In my test, I killed the session on the remote, saw the new ssh connection, but I started seeing this error repeated in the terminal where i started sshuttle: 

```
TypeError: compile() expected string without null bytes
Traceback (most recent call last):

> However, I now have a different question -- Is it possible to mark certain tests as never run in parallel?

There's nothing builtin for that, unfortunately. We will probably discuss something to the effect in the upcoming [pytest sprint]() though. 

We have the same problem here at work, specially for some sensitive GUI tests (@gabrielcnr will know what I'm talking about), and we solve it like this:

1. Mark tests which can't execute in parallel with some mark, say `serial`:

  ```python
  @pytest.mark.serial
  def test_snt(self):
        assert True
  ```

2. Execute pytest twice: once executing in parallel but **excluding** those with the mark, and then executing it again serially but executing only the tests **with** the mark:
  
  ```
  $ py.test -n4 -m "not serial"
  ...
  $ py.test -m serial
  ```

It works well for us. :grin:

@yonjah Like I said, big bang deployment is not a solution for everybody. https deployment is always a tightrope walk between security and usability. If Google would follow your argumentation, they would drop RC4 based ssl ciphers. But, they want to support legacy systems and still support insecure ciphers (https://www.ssllabs.com/ssltest/analyze.html?d=www.google.com&hideResults=on). You as a security focused person are often not the sole policy maker. This is especially true if you are responsible for a company website where the company managers, important customers etc. have a say, too.

> The thing is @dasa123 , if you can afford to initiate insecure connection if ssl fails why can't you just do it for everyone else and drop ssl support completely?

You can grant website visitors different levels of usage rights, e.g.:
- Windows XP without https support => Leave them on http and inform them to switch their operating system or at least move to current Firefox version. Disable "login" and "register" functionality.
- Windows XP with Firefox => Redirect to https and inform them to use https in the future via HSTS
- Modern systems => Allow only https

> And if the data going on the line is half critical, how can you afford to compromise users just to have more traffic ? I think security best practice learned us that if you can't serve on a secure connection you should fail the connection with as much bells and whistles. It is in the user best interest not to interact with your site if the connection is not secure.

You might not be the sole decission maker. The company might earn their money with the website... 
What do you think is the reason for amazon.com still supporting http alongside https?

> And you are right that https-only is only making one link in the security chain stronger, but I don't see how this is a case for making it weaker.

https is flawed by nature. Just because you allow access only over https doesn't mean that the connection can't be "disabled by untrusted certificate" (https://github.com/letsencrypt/letsencrypt/issues/1660#issuecomment-201545991). A recent case: https://security.googleblog.com/2015/03/maintaining-digital-certificate-security.html

> I looked into marshmallow-jsonapi and understand how include data is gathered and how that can generate lot's of queries.

Are your model relationships being lazy loaded? The queries will not fire until marshmallow-jsonapi accesses the model attribute/relationship belonging to it. If you are constantly accessing the same relationships on a request, it would make sense to load the relationships with the parent.

1. Get a [benchmark](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/faq/performance.html) for each include relationship query.
2. Change the [relationship loading technique](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/orm/loading_relationships.html).
3. Add appropriate [indexes](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/constraints.html?highlight=index#indexes) for your attributes and relationship foreign keys.

@rob-c I just mean that we can run the Shutdown 'manually'. In the example above, you wouldn't get a full tearDown because the atexit stuff isn't being called so the decorator would supply that in whatever way is currently done when the `atexit` handlers **are** called. I realise this is something that GangaTestCase does do in theory but it seems that is having problems and also that would be the only reason to keep GangaTestCase over the normal Python test stuff. If the Ganga shutdown code can be in one (and only one) place and just called when needed in a test, then we should be able to simplify things.

Now, the point about doing a full shutdown after every test is up for debate. Personally, I feel that most (but certainly not all) tests don't need the full shutdown and in fact shouldn't care about it. If all you want to do is some unit tests or basic GPI tests, you shouldn't need to fully shutdown everything. However, I can see a reason for doing this as well. Anyway, none of this is happening soon anyway so we can discuss and come to a consensus.

I started looking into adding summary.xml / metadata support to GaudiExec.

The first part is to make the job produce `summary.xml` and ship it to the local host. This part is trivial and it works.

The second point is the parsing and merging of the xmls. For old applications, the parsing was done in the WN and the output was shipped back. There is a good reason for this -- the parsing relies on being in the LHCb software environment. I *think* we should keep this behaviour. 

The more complicated thing is merging in case we have a job with subjobs. Again, to merge the xmls and produce the merged metadata, you need to be in the LHCb environment. `execCmd()` comes to mind immediately. However, it relies on the existence of the project source directory. Here is the deeper issue that there is no guarantee that the source directory exists. I may have made a temporary lb-dev project just to pick up a released app version with no local additions. Then I delete it and next time I run ganga, `app.directory` points to a non-existing directory.

I have two solutions in mind
1. Run the merging script with `lb-run LHCb <version> ...`, where version can be "latest" or a fixed number.
2. Modify `execCmd()` to run from the tar-ed project. I'm not sure if there is a better way than untar-ing on every call. I've implemented that and is there is no perceivable delay for a project with no modifications. Currently, I've changed the signature to `execCmd(self, cmd, use_source=False)` to allow for the use case from `buildGangaTarget()`.

Even if you decide to go for 1, 2 is a something to consider as missing (or modified!) source directory it is a problem on its own.

Hmmm. This might be an acceptable use case, but I'm not sure it makes sense here. After all, the public bucket is only editable by a very few people in ops -- it's not the staging bucket, for instance. I don't think it's dangerous to leak that information, but maybe I'm wrong. Even with the staging bucket, I kind of wonder what the value is in keeping that information secret. Mozilla "works in the open". Anyhow, I think there are lots of use cases where a user who doesn't have write permissions ought to be able to see *something* about the object's permissions. For example, let's say I'm looking at a record that I have read permissions to -- right now I don't even know what permissions *I* have on that object. I think it makes sense for me to be able to know whether my friend has read permissions to it as well. Google Drive, for instance, lets anyone with any level of access to an object see who the owner of the object is and who else has permissions on the object.

I think what makes this feel unsafe is the fact that it's an email address rather than a semi-opaque user name. Maybe this is a good use case for the accounts plugin? Then we could at least hide the email address behind a semi-opaque userid. (Of course, that userid is only one step away from an email address, but still...)

TabularInline is not working at all. Jet override includes/fieldset.html with this: field.field|jet_select2_lookups, but TabularInline doesn't include this fieldset.html. 

You can override tabular.html with field.field|jet_select2_lookups, but there is something error here. In jet_select2_lookups tag, you can find the below line:

            initial_value = form.data.get(field.name) if form.data != {} else form.initial.get(field.name)

In a inline formset, if form.data != {}, form.data.get(field.name) will get nothing. For the field.name is like xx, but the form data will contains formset_prefix-n-xx.

I override this line with 
initial_value = form.data.get(field.html_name) if form.data != {} else form.initial.get(field.name)

ええっと一応ちゃんと説明すると私が実装しようとするバージョンでは最初のif文の条件式にない信号をリセットとして認識しません。同期非同期問わず。
なので上記のようなコードはRST_XはMEM_WEにとってリセットではないと認識され、senslistに追加されます。
ところが、これはbindvisitor._createalwaysinfoに前からあったコードですが、
```python
        if clock_edge is not None and len(senslist) > 0:
            raise verror.FormatError('Illegal sensitivity list')
```
という記述があり、ここで例外になってしまいます。そこで、一つのalwaysブロック内に複数の信号への代入が存在する場合、どこかでリセットとして使われていれば例外としない、というような処理に変えようと思ってます。

もう一つ、現状framesのalwaysinfoは各alwaysブロックごとに一つのCLK/RST情報を保持していますが、RST_XをMEM_WEのリセットにさせないために、一つのalwaysのなかで複数のCLK/RST情報を持たせる変更をします。

travis終わったらこの方針で実装しますが、イメージと違うなあと思ったら止めてください。

Hi Joey

Feel free to try any better solution. My main concern is to not bloat the
repo with notebooks, plus all the various commit history of the notebooks
since each Jupyter notebook change can be big. It might be the same if the
HTML is just 50% of the executed notebook. Certainly small HTML files are
fine but a 20MB HTML is excessive ...

One potential workaround is something like this:

- keep the unexecuted notebooks in matminer repo
- have a script that executes that notebooks and pushes the HTML to a gist,
e.g., under the hackingmaterials organization.
- Link to the hackingmaterials gists in the docs.

Note that there might be a use case to still link to external gists. e.g.
if someone has some cool example of matminer, we can link to it without
putting in the main repo (doing the latter means that we need to support
that example for all time). But, it makes sense to have some core examples
within matminer.

Best,
Anubhav

class UserNode(DjangoNode):

Now I do see some lineages using Apache authenticator in the `--force-renewal` log file. Just to note: be careful while running `--force-renewal` against the production server, as you might run into the Let's Encrypt limits at some point.

Now, I do believe I see what's the issue there;

You seem to be using various authenticators across the certificate lineages on a single system. This might probably is the issue. I would suggest setting up a single scheme for validation on said box. I'll give a short rundown of what is the probable issue. Let's ignore use case of running renewal for a single certificate here for a bit, and focus on the problematic scenario. A bit of background here about the `--pre-hook` and `--post-hook`; the pre-hook runs before ANY challenges, and post-hook runs after EVERY challenge has been done. 

Now when you have a scenario, where two certificates need renewal, one of which uses Apache plugin as authenticator, and another one that uses standalone. Here's a rundown of execution order to give you a picture what I see as the probable cause. In this scenario Certbot is run with pre hook stopping Apache and a post hook starting it:

1. Certbot runs pre-hook stopping Apache service
2. The certificate using Apache plugin is renewed, this restarts Apache during the challenge validation, and leaves it running as Apache plugin should do.
3. The certificate using standalone plugin is renewed. Apache is running, so standalone is unable to bind to ip addresses, making it fail. Now I see that you are using IPv6 at least part of your stack, so the outcome actually depends of the fact of which addresses Apache is configured to bind itself to. So if Apache binds only to IPv4, standalone might still be able to bind IPv6. 
4. Certbot runs a post-hook trying to start Apache

Because of the varying outcome of step 3 it's really hard to actually figure out the root cause of the issue. Let's Encrypt prefers IPv6 over IPv4, so if your domain has an AAAA record, that's what Let's Encrypt always tries to connect to. 

Now the different methods of challenge validation, both needing to bind to ports 80 or 443, one of which is supposed to stay bound after the challenge, and with pre- and post hooks affecting the outcome I feel that it would make a lot of sense to figure out and configure a single method of challenge validation that will be used across all the certificates in tihs one box. This will save you a lot of headaches in the future.

Here I suffered from the same problem that due to incorrect private key permissions my sendmail STARTTLS support suddenly stopped working after renewal of my certificates. Here I have now adapted my daily cronjob to do the following:

```
MAILTO="root"
0 */12 * * * root /usr/bin/certbot renew --text >>/var/log/certbot.log 2>&1 || (find /etc/letsencrypt/archive -type f -name "privkey*.pem" -exec chmod 600 {} \; ; /etc/init.d/nginx reload; /etc/init.d/dovecot reload; /etc/init.d/sendmail restart)
```

Thus, I will enforce proper 600 permissions after successful execution of certbot. This temporary solved my issues until certbot itself supports to set file permissions correctly.

I have traced this issue to the following part of cleanPath:

```python
                if cmd == 'm':
                    # remove m0,0 segments
                    if pathIndex > 0 and data[0] == data[i + 1] == 0:
                        # 'm0,0 x,y' can be replaces with 'lx,y',
                        # except the first m which is a required absolute moveto
                        path[pathIndex] = ('l', data[2:])
                        _num_path_segments_removed += 1
                    else:  # else skip move coordinate
                        i = 2
```

The bug disappears if we special case on "len(data) == 2" and insist on keeping the "m0,0" command in that case.  However, that breaks trips the following test case:

```python
class PathEmptyMove(unittest.TestCase):

    def runTest(self):
        doc = scourXmlFile('unittests/path-empty-move.svg')
        self.assertEqual(doc.getElementsByTagName('path')[0].getAttribute('d'), 'm100 100 200 100z')
        self.assertEqual(doc.getElementsByTagName('path')[1].getAttribute('d'), 'm100 100v200l100 100z')
```

Test data:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <path d="m100 100 l200 100 m0 0z" />
  <path d="m100 100 v200 m0 0 100 100z" />
</svg>
```

The first path ends with "m0 0z" and the test case expects this to be reduced into "z" (the second path should be unaffected by this change).  From what I gathered from the [SVG 1.1 standard], the "m0 0" will create a new subpath and "z" will close the current subpath (i.e. draw a lone from the current position to the beginning of the subpath).  I.e. a "m0 0z" is basically a no op (or a dot; not quite sure which).  

As I understand it, this makes "Xm0 0z" and "Xz" are two different things.  The former creates "X" and then an "empty" subpath while the latter creates X and then closes it by drawing a straight line from the current position to the initial point of X.  In other words, if I understood the spec correctly the current test is wrong (it should either leave "m0 0z" alone or also remove the "z"; not entirely sure which of the two are correct though).

If we agree on that point, I will look into providing a patch for this bug.  Alternatively, I will need help figuring out when "m0 0" can be removed and when it cannot.

[SVG 1.1 standard]: https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/paths.html

@AdriaanRol We have now thought about this issue in the sense that we encountered the same situation in some drivers we wrote. For instance, the ZI UHF-LI sweeper needs to be configured (sweep start, sweep stop, number of points, filter bandwidth, etc.) before the `get` method of the sweeper parameter can be called without raising an exception. This is, as you pointed out, a quite general situation and always (but not limited to) the case for non-scalar parameters. The way we solve it is by having a  boolean class attribute called something like `sweep_correctly_configured`. All relevant parameters then falsify that boolean in their `set` methods and some `sweep.build_sweep()` method must be called to set it back to `True` (and actually send the configurations to the instrument). This seems similar to the signal hound solution, which I personaly think is a fine way of doing things. 

Have you yourself thought more about this in the meantime? I don't really see the advantage of promoting this bookkeeping to the instrument class. What would the benefit be over what we can call the signal hound approach?

Coincidentally and more FYI at this point, I've been working on a libssh2 based client as an option for future releases which will also avoid the need to monkey patch. Libssh2 has significantly higher performance than paramiko and a solid, tested code base.

Out of interest, what use case do you have for paramiko 2? It will work if you upgrade it manually, parallel-ssh is API compatible with it, but you might run into regressions.

I think that's what the motivation was, i.e., our dates were timezone unaware, so we make all dates with reference to UTC.

The problem seems to happen when a partial date is specified when typing.
